main_ingredients: grass jelly extract (Platostoma palustre / Mesona chinensis)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged grass jelly drink is one can or bottle of approximately 300 ml, equivalent to 300 g, providing about 117 kcal and 27 g of sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Grass Jelly Drink (Packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged grass jelly drink (cr_id D05010019). Confidence 0.95 as reported in source metadata.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged grass jelly drink in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the closest analogous food: sugar-sweetened herbal/plant-based beverage with similar sugar content (~9g/100g sucrose-dominant). Sucrose GI is approximately 65; dilution in a beverage matrix with starch and plant solids lowers effective GI to approximately 50–55. GL per 300g serving = (52 × 27.0g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 14.0, rounded to 14.2. This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this product is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.08g/100g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 25mg/100g but does not offset SSB risk.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid packaged sweetened grass jelly drinks because the 9.08g/100g sugar load in liquid form independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, regardless of the low sodium content; unsweetened grass jelly preparations are a suitable alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.08g sugar/100g and 0g dietary fibre; liquid sugar raises postprandial glucose rapidly with no fibre buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should avoid this packaged grass jelly drink entirely, as it is a sugar-sweetened beverage delivering 9.08g sugar per 100g with zero dietary fibre, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; unsweetened grass jelly (xiancao) without added sugar is the recommended alternative per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk through dyslipidaemia, insulin resistance, and elevated triglycerides; no protective nutrients present.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this packaged grass jelly drink, as habitual sugar-sweetened beverage consumption (9.08g sugar/100g, 0g fibre, no omega-3 or antioxidant nutrients) is independently associated with increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines; plain water or unsweetened herbal drinks are preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 9.08g/100g with no anti-inflammatory nutrients promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this packaged grass jelly drink as the 9.08g/100g added sugar content promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production with no offsetting anti-inflammatory nutrients; unsweetened grass jelly retains herbal polyphenol properties and is a suitable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged grass jelly drink is a ready-to-drink (RTD) sweetened herbal beverage widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It is made from grass jelly extract derived from Platostoma palustre (also known as Mesona chinensis or xiancao in Chinese, cincau in Malay), a plant in the mint family traditionally used in Chinese herbal medicine. The commercial packaged version is produced by dissolving or suspending grass jelly extract in water with added sugar and starch, then filling into cans or bottles for retail sale. The drink has a mild, slightly bitter herbal flavour with a sweet finish from added sugar, and a thin to slightly viscous texture. Per 100g, it provides 39 kcal, 9.54g carbohydrate (of which 9.08g is sugar), 0g protein, 0g fat, 0g dietary fibre, and 25mg sodium. Clinically, this product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high liquid sugar content and absence of fibre, protein, or protective micronutrients. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low-medium boundary) based on analogous SSB data, but the glycemic load per 300ml serving is approximately 14, which is clinically meaningful. Unsweetened grass jelly preparations retain some herbal polyphenol properties and are a preferred alternative for health-conscious consumers. This food is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable.
common_user_queries: Is grass jelly drink good for diabetes?
Can I drink packaged grass jelly drink if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in a can of grass jelly drink?
Is packaged grass jelly drink suitable for weight loss?
What is the sugar content of grass jelly drink?
What is the difference between packaged grass jelly drink and fresh grass jelly?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 as reported in source metadata. The food is an exact match in the HPB database (cr_id D05010019). GI is an analogous estimate as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — this is a water-based herbal beverage with no protein-contributing ingredients by formulation. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — no fat-containing ingredients are present in the standard formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — the grass jelly extract in the packaged commercial form contributes negligible fibre. calcium_mg and iron_mg are confirmed zero per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-based beverage with no animal-derived ingredients. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for packaged grass jelly drink are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this product as of 2026. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened herbal beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI testing of this specific product is recommended. The input record listed the condition key as 'diabetes' which has been updated to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from input cultural metadata are noted: sodium at 25mg/100mg qualifies as low sodium; energy at 39 kcal/100g qualifies as low calorie, however the product is still classified as an SSB due to sugar content.
